title: Mesa-Optimization is Destroying Education
gist: Applying the AI-alignment concept of mesa-optimization to Campbell's Law, the author argues American education's metrics (GPA, SAT) have been systematically gamed into meaninglessness, and proposes that AI-native, state-mandated, non-augmentative assessment systems (modeled on Alpha School) could restore honest signal by moving the gaming to a more visible, auditable level.

insights.claim: Campbell's Law holds that any quantitative indicator used for social decision-making becomes a target for manipulation, and as pressure to hit that target grows, the measure loses its connection to the reality it was meant to track.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Any KPI-driven system (corporate, regulatory, academic) that lacks a correction mechanism should be assumed to decay toward gaming over time; treat headline metrics from long-incentivized systems with skepticism.
insights.quote: will distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor.
insights.locator: In 1976 the social scientist and cyberneticist Donald T. Campbell

insights.claim: Campbell's original 1970s examples included prosecutors misusing plea bargains to pin unsolved crimes on defendants to inflate clearance rates, and U.S. Vietnam War commanders inflating enemy-killed counts (including counting civilian dead as KIA) to hit body-count benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Historical precedent for how any single quantitative success metric imposed on an institution under pressure gets gamed rather than genuinely improved.
insights.quote: commanders inflating those numbers and even including civilian dead as KIA.
insights.locator: He pointed out that during the Vietnam War

insights.claim: The SAT itself has been progressively degraded as a measurement tool: long literary reading passages were replaced with short excerpts and vocabulary-based questions were removed, so simply reinstating standardized testing will not by itself fix the evaluation problem.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Watch for policy narratives that assume 'bringing back the SAT' is a fix; the test itself needs to be reformed, not just reinstated, for admissions signal to recover.
insights.locator: Today, the same institutional pressures that led UCSD

insights.claim: Mesa-optimization, a term from the 2019 alignment paper 'Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems' (co-authored by researchers from Eliezer Yudkowsky's Machine Intelligence Research Institute), describes a system a 'base optimizer' trains toward one objective that ends up running an internal search toward a different, unspecified 'mesa-objective' — the 'inner alignment problem.'
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: This AI-alignment vocabulary gives investors and policymakers a portable diagnostic for spotting when any large system (institution, company incentive plan, regulatory regime) has drifted from its stated mandate toward a locally-optimized proxy.
insights.quote: where a meta-optimizer sits one level above the system it tunes, a mesa-optimizer sits one level below.
insights.locator: How Signal Gets Distorted

insights.claim: Any large human institution has a designed base objective plus a downstream web of mesa-objectives that emerge from incentive and survival pressures on the people inside it, and Campbell's Law is the mechanism by which unchecked mesa-optimization distorts the base objective.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A generalizable lens: when analyzing any institution's stated mission versus its actual behavior, look for the emergent local incentives (mesa-objectives) driving staff, not just the formal charter.
insights.locator: In any large human institution we can identify a base objective

insights.claim: The author uses natural selection and humans as an analogy: natural selection (the 'base process') optimizing for reproduction produced humans, who as mesa-optimizers now pursue goals like celibacy, contraception, and adoption that are actively misaligned with the base objective of reproduction.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Illustrates that mesa-optimization can produce agents whose objectives fully diverge from, not just drift from, the base objective — a useful mental model for how far institutional incentives can decouple from founding purpose.
insights.locator: The process of natural selection, for example

themes.theme: Evidence of US Education's Signal Collapse

insights.claim: A 2025 UC San Diego report found roughly one in eight incoming freshmen tested below high school level in math and nearly 12 percent of the entering class enrolled in remedial courses, even though the average high school math GPA of students placed in the lowest remedial track was an A-minus.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete, recent metric showing the gap between credentialed and actual competence at a selective public university — a leading indicator for the scale of grade inflation nationally.
insights.quote: roughly one in eight incoming freshmen tested below high school level in math, with nearly 12 percent of the entering class enrolling in remedial courses.
insights.locator: At UC San Diego, a 2025 report found

insights.claim: UCSD's remedial-math surge followed its decision to drop standardized testing as an admissions measure, removing one of the few tools that had counteracted high-school grade inflation.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Directly links test-optional admissions policy to a measurable decline in incoming student preparedness at a specific institution.
insights.locator: In UCSD's case, these results came after standardized testing was dropped

insights.claim: College GPAs have risen at roughly a tenth of a point per decade, and an A is now the most commonly awarded grade, close to 43 percent of all grades given, versus far rarer a generation earlier.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Quantifies the pace of grade inflation as a long, steady trend rather than a recent AI-era phenomenon, meaning LLMs are accelerating, not causing, an existing structural problem.
insights.quote: close to 43 percent of the total
insights.locator: College GPAs began rising at roughly a tenth of a point per decade

insights.claim: Between 2010 and 2021 average U.S. high school GPA climbed from 3.17 to 3.36 even as average ACT scores fell to their lowest point of the decade, a direct divergence between reported and demonstrated performance.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A clean, dateable divergence between two competing signals (self-reported GPA vs. standardized test) that investors in ed-tech or credentialing should treat as the smoking-gun data point for the thesis.
insights.locator: Between 2010 and 2021 the average high school GPA climbed

insights.claim: In 1995 the College Board 'recentered' the SAT scale to bring the national average back to 500 per section, instantly adding roughly eighty points to the typical verbal score and twenty points to the math score without any underlying improvement in ability.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Shows that score inflation can be administratively engineered overnight by the test administrator itself, not just gamed bottom-up by students and schools.
insights.locator: In 1995, the College Board “recentered” the SAT scale

insights.claim: By the 2020s the test-optional movement had spread to the great majority of four-year colleges, with the University of California system dropping the SAT and ACT entirely, further eroding a standardized check on grade inflation.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Marks the institutional tipping point at which the last broad, comparable competence signal in U.S. admissions was largely abandoned.
insights.locator: By the 2020s the test-optional movement had spread

insights.claim: Gamed disability accommodations such as extended test time have further inflated scores, and over a third of undergraduates at elite academic institutions now hold disability accommodations, which the author frames as part of an expensive, humiliating signaling arms race.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A provocative, specific statistic (over a third of elite undergrads) that signals how far credential-gaming has spread into adjacent systems (disability accommodation processes) beyond grades and tests themselves.
insights.quote: over a third of undergraduates at elite academic institutions have disability accommodations.
insights.locator: And it means breaking the expensive and humiliating signaling arms race

insights.claim: Hannah Arendt argued in 1958 that America's education crisis was not fundamentally a pedagogy problem but a symptom of a broader modern crisis of authority, in which adults no longer felt entitled to present the world to the young and take responsibility for it.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Reframes the education-metrics problem as ultimately a governance/accountability problem, which is why the author's proposed fix centers on restoring auditable adult judgment, not just better tests.
insights.quote: the crisis in American education was not an education problem at all, but a fragment of a modern crisis of authority.
insights.locator: In 1958 Hannah Arendt argued

insights.claim: Progressive pedagogical theory (associated with John Dewey's 'adjustment learning') held that students form an autonomous society that should largely govern itself, which relieved adults of the need to exercise judgment and authority and set the stage for later grade inflation.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Traces the ideological root of decades of grade inflation to a specific pedagogical doctrine, useful for identifying which institutions or reform movements are downstream of the same root cause.
insights.locator: Reformers, she claimed, posited that there exists a

insights.claim: Education was historically a rare, elite pursuit: Harvard (founded 1636) trained clergy and gentry, only about 3.5 percent of college-age Americans were enrolled in higher education by 1900, and still only around 7 percent by 1940.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline against which the scale of the later mass-education expansion, and its incentive distortions, should be measured.
insights.locator: Historically, education was a virtuous pursuit venerated by elites

insights.claim: The GI Bill of 1944 and the Higher Education Act of 1965 opened mass enrollment and federal student aid, which has underwritten ballooning tuition costs and rapid institutional expansion ever since.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific federal policy origin points of the tuition-inflation and enrollment-growth dynamics still shaping higher-ed economics today, relevant to any thesis on student-loan or tuition policy.
insights.locator: The GI Bill of 1944 then sent millions of returning veterans

insights.claim: Civil Rights-era politicization of unequal outcomes and access, combined with financial pressure to reduce attrition and later a focus on 'student satisfaction,' pushed universities away from pedagogy and toward customer-service objectives, with administrators adopting 'success rate' language by the late 1980s as if it were the base objective itself.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Pinpoints the historical moment (late 1980s) when university administration's implicit objective function shifted from education to retention/satisfaction metrics — the root mesa-objective the article argues corrupted the system.
insights.locator: By the late 1980s a new vocabulary had taken hold

themes.theme: Labor Market Response and Human Capital Risk

insights.claim: As GPAs and SAT scores became unreliable, employers began relying on alternative, more expensive signals such as internship history and public GitHub or project portfolios, since these costly signals still carried information the original cheap signals had stopped conveying.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A structural tailwind for platforms and services built around portfolio-based or work-sample hiring signals (e.g., project-based credentialing, GitHub-style proof-of-work) as a substitute for degraded academic credentials.
insights.locator: In response, employers struggling to discern high-quality candidates

insights.claim: The author predicts that as primary academic signals keep collapsing, admissions and hiring will increasingly lean on socioeconomically-correlated indicators like essays, extracurriculars, and philanthropy, which risk entrenching privilege rather than measuring competence.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Flags a second-order equity risk: credential decay doesn't just create noise, it shifts selection toward proxies that favor wealthier applicants, a relevant consideration for education-policy and access-focused investors.
insights.locator: Employers look to internships and project portfolios

insights.claim: Contrary to the common assumption that a degrading education system produces clean stratification (with the smartest/hardest-working rewarded proportionally), the author argues the more likely outcome is broad-based competence decline, where intelligent outlier students only need a 'mediocre level of output' to surpass mediocre peers, so top institutions still capture 'top talent' by label without genuine competence diffusing into the broader workforce.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A contrarian human-capital thesis: don't assume top-decile talent pools remain as strong as their credentials suggest — even elite cohorts may be underperforming their labels, a risk for employers and investors relying on pedigree as a competence proxy.
insights.quote: Intelligent, outlier students will be rewarded for a mediocre level of output that is sufficient to surpass that of their peers.
insights.locator: This has been commonly described as a stratification process

themes.theme: AI as a Mesa-Resistant Fix: The Alpha School Case

insights.claim: Absent external pressure, the education sector will only get incremental 'AI integration' from incumbent ed-tech vendors who profit from the current dysfunctional system and have no incentive to expose the gap between credentials and actual competence.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A direct warning against expecting disruption from existing ed-tech incumbents; real signal-restoring innovation is more likely to come from new entrants or state mandates than from established LMS/assessment vendors.
insights.locator: Built properly, AI educational systems could focus on the base objective

insights.claim: Alpha School, an Austin-based private school, runs instruction and assessment primarily through a proprietary AI system that generates endlessly varied assessments to prevent gaming and requires 90 percent accuracy before a student can unlock new material, evaluating uniformly across classrooms and districts.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete, named, currently-operating case study of AI-native mastery-based assessment that investors/operators can benchmark against — its scoring distributions and item difficulty are described as publishable and auditable.
insights.quote: require 90 percent accuracy before unlocking new material at the student’s own pace.
insights.locator: Alpha School is an Austin-based private school

insights.claim: Alpha School's human 'guides' (who replace teachers) were revealed in 2025 to mostly consist of remote workers from the Philippines, which the author says completes rather than solves Arendt's abdication of adult responsibility, even while the AI layer solves the measurement problem.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A specific operational red flag for anyone evaluating or investing in the Alpha School model: solving assessment integrity does not automatically solve the mentorship/authority gap, and current staffing choices may undercut the model's credibility.
insights.quote: revealed to mostly consist of remote workers from the Philippines
insights.locator: In 2025 they were revealed to mostly consist of remote workers

insights.claim: AI-based assessment is more resistant to Campbell's Law-style gaming not because it can't be gamed, but because the gaming moves up a level — to administrators choosing difficulty settings and thresholds — making it far more visible and auditable to parents, teachers, and other stakeholders than a teacher quietly inflating grades behind closed doors.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: The core mechanism of the article's proposed fix: transparency of gaming, not elimination of gaming, is what restores signal — a generalizable design principle for any AI-driven measurement system meant to resist institutional capture.
insights.quote: AI is more resistant, precisely because the gaming moves up a level, and at that level it becomes more visible to parents, teachers, and other interested parties.
insights.locator: The relevant question, however, is whether or not AI is more resistant

insights.claim: State-mandated 'model cards' publishing difficulty calibrations, the underlying teaching corpus, and expected score distributions for median students would let anyone see when a district chooses an easy setting to boost pass rates, unlike current opaque grading.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete, implementable policy proposal (AI model cards for education) that regulators or state education agencies could adopt directly.
insights.locator: State-mandated model cards could publish difficulty calibrations

insights.claim: Transparent, common-standard AI assessment would enable upward mobility that the current system blocks: a student in a low-rigor district who aces every assessment could get an individualized difficulty upgrade from their parents without switching schools, because capability becomes visible against a shared standard rather than hidden behind an uninterpretable local grade.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A social-mobility upside case for AI assessment standardization that goes beyond fixing signal noise — it could unlock talent currently trapped in low-rigor districts.
insights.locator: This transparency also enables upward mobility

insights.claim: Continuous AI micro-quizzing woven into daily instruction would generate a time series per student, revealing inflection points and exposing which teachers consistently produce upward learning trajectories versus stagnation, eliminating the current excuse that a teacher merely 'inherited weak students.'
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Describes a specific accountability mechanism (longitudinal per-student AI data) that could underpin teacher-evaluation and school-accountability systems, relevant to ed-tech products building continuous-assessment infrastructure.
insights.locator: Under the current system, a struggling teacher can plausibly claim

insights.claim: The author insists AI assessment cannot be bolted onto existing testing regimes or learning-management systems as an add-on; it must be a strategic state-level commitment implemented as a full replacement of existing assessment infrastructure, or the current system's distortions will simply absorb and neutralize it.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A clear filter for evaluating ed-tech 'AI integration' announcements: incremental add-ons to legacy systems should be treated skeptically as unlikely to fix signal integrity, per the article's own thesis.
insights.quote: The current system’s distortions would simply absorb and neutralize it.
insights.locator: What Alpha School gets right is that none of this can be bolted on

insights.claim: The author predicts real implementation requires a state with unusual political cover — a governor with political capital to spend, or a jurisdiction desperate enough to try something because it is far behind — and speculates it might not happen in the U.S. first.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Suggests investors/policy-watchers should look for early adoption signals in specific U.S. states with strong executive political capital, or even in non-U.S. jurisdictions, rather than assuming federal or coastal-elite leadership.
insights.locator: It requires a state with unusual political cover

insights.claim: Honest AI-based measurement will initially crater GPAs and spike failure rates, imposing a short-term political cost on whoever implements it even though the long-term benefit (restored trust in credentials) diffuses across the whole economy — a classic collective-action problem that discourages any single actor from moving first.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Explains why reform is politically hard despite broad economic benefit — a useful framework for assessing the probability and timing of any state-level rollout.
insights.locator: Planners need to accept that honest measurement

insights.claim: The author flags that Alpha School students perform well on Measures of Academic Progress (MAP) tests, but warns that relying solely on this single metric risks the same Campbell's Law dynamic the article critiques, and it will take time to see how these students actually perform in higher education and the workforce.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A self-aware caveat: even the article's flagship proof-of-concept could itself be gaming a single external benchmark, so its long-run outcomes (college/career performance) are the real test still pending.
insights.locator: Alpha students perform extremely well on Measures of Academic Progress

insights.claim: The author warns that interacting with a sycophantic AI model can damage a growing mind and risks contributing to 'AI psychosis,' meaning human feedback remains necessary to instill mental discipline even in an AI-driven assessment system, with teachers' future role redefined toward observation and mentorship rather than grading.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A risk flag for any fully AI-mediated education model: removing teacher-graders without preserving a strong human mentorship function creates a distinct psychological/developmental risk, not just an accountability gap.
insights.locator: Interacting with a sycophantic AI model can be damaging

insights.claim: The author explicitly proposes generalizing the mesa-optimization/Campbell's Law framework beyond education to other 'intractable problems' such as community safety, healthcare, and purportedly unbiased information systems like journalism, arguing these could similarly benefit from unbiased AI-based measurement to restore signal propagation.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Points investors and policy analysts toward adjacent sectors (healthcare quality metrics, criminal-justice performance measures, media/journalism bias auditing) as candidates for the same AI-driven, mesa-optimization-resistant measurement thesis.
insights.locator: We can apply this framework to some of our other intractable problems
